source_metadata.title_raw: Alan B. Fenstermacher, Esq. - A-19-020 - March 15, 2019 - Costa Mesa

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 15, 2019

Alan B. Fenstermacher, Esq.
Rutan & Tucker, LLP
611 Anton Boulevard, 14th Floor
Costa Mesa, CA 92626

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-020

Dear Mr. Fenstermacher:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Poway Deputy Mayor
Dave Grosch and Councilmembers Barry Leonard and John Mullin regarding the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act ("Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under
the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest
prohibitions, such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not
the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTION

May Deputy Mayor Grosch and Councilmembers Leonard and Mullin participate in
decisions to supplement two Landscape Maintenance Districts' ("LMD") budgets by paying for
some landscaping-related services or improvements within the LMDs out of the City's General
Fund, when the Deputy Mayor Grosch lives approximately 1,000 feet from the closest potential
landscape change, Councilmember Leonard lives within 500 feet from the closest potential
landscape change, and Councilmember Mullin lives more than 500 feet but less than 1,000 feet
from the closest potential landscape change.

CONCLUSION

Yes – it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material effect on the
Councilmembers' financial interest in their residences.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-020
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

The City of Poway has five landscape maintenance districts ("LMDs"). These LMDs
establish and maintain special assessments on all the parcels within their jurisdictional boundaries.
The City uses those funds to maintain landscaping improvements in common areas within the
LMD, including along large arterial streets.

Due to increasing maintenance costs since the LMDs were formed decades ago, the existing
special assessments in a couple of these LMDs are no longer sufficient to maintain an acceptable
level of landscaping, even though the City also makes a defined General Fund contribution to all
LMDs. Thus, in 2018, the City proposed reengineering two LMDs – LMD 83-1 and LMD 86-1 –
subject to a protest vote. The proposed reengineering would have altered the assessment amounts
and the number of properties in each LMD, resulting in a net positive income to each LMD.
However, in both cases, a majority protest was received, and these two LMDs remain in the same
form as when they were originally established in the 1980s. Yearly special assessments on the tax
bills of the real estate owners in these two districts currently range from $0 on certain properties to
$496.16.

Considering the two majority protest votes, the City Council plans to consider whether
to spend additional General Fund money for landscape maintenance purposes in LMDs 83-1 and
86-1. If the City Council decides not to contribute additional General Fund money, several large
eucalyptus trees would likely need to be removed as they die, as the current level of watering, based
on the current funds, is not sufficient to support all the existing trees. If the City Council
appropriates additional General Fund money, it is very unlikely additional trees would be added.
Instead, the money would be spent preserving existing trees by increasing watering levels and
perhaps enhancing ground level improvements.

In sum, the City Council's options are likely (i) no additional General Fund money and
stumping trees (ii) some additional General Fund money to keep existing trees alive, and (iii) even
more General Fund money for additional enhancements that would assist the LMD with longevity,
such as drought tolerant improvements, but not additional trees. Any decision the City Council
makes will not impact the special assessments.

The City plans to schedule a workshop for the City Council on this topic at its March 19
2019 City Council meeting, where the City Council will provide staff with input, without taking any
formal action. While the City Council will not be taking formal action at this workshop, at a future
meeting, the City Council will likely consider and take formal action on staff recommendations
formulated based on public and Councilmember input received at this workshop.

Three City Councilmembers are potentially impacted when the City Council acts with
respect to LMDs 83-1 and 86-1. Deputy Mayor and Councilmember Dave Grosch lives
approximately 1,000 feet from the closest potential landscape change, Councilmember Leonard
lives within 500 feet from the closest potential landscape change, and Councilmember Mullin lives
more than 500 feet but less than 1,000 feet from the closest potential landscape change. Each
Councilmember owns their residence.

File No. A-19-020
Page No. 3

Many of the landscaping improvements in both LMDs 83-1 and 86-1 are not located within
the residential subdivisions themselves, but rather on widely-used City arterials. The landscape
improvements do not have a unique impact on any of the Councilmembers' residences when
compared to any other property located in or near their respective LMDs. To some degree, many
citizens of the City benefit from these landscaping improvements when driving down these arterial
streets.

The potential landscape changes are not located adjacent to the Councilmembers'
residences, are not visible from the residences, nor are the improvements located within the actual
residential subdivisions where the Councilmembers reside. Instead, the improvements that will be
affected by the City Council's action(s) are almost entirely located on larger arterial streets, outside
the actual neighborhoods themselves.

ANALYSIS

Under Section 87100, a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes,
participates in making, or uses his or her position to influence a governmental decision which has a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests.

Section 87103(b) indicates that an official has a financial interest in "[a]ny real property in
which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or
more." Section 87103 also indicates that an official has a financial interest in his or her "personal
finances, including those of his or her immediate family, also known as the 'personal financial
effects rule.'"² We now consider the financial effect of the decisions at issue on the officials'
respective real property interests.

Foreseeability:

The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly
involved in the decision. (Regulation 18701.) An interest is explicitly involved in a decision if the
interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the governmental decision. (Regulation 18701(a).)
Under the facts you have provided, the officials' properties are not explicitly involved in the
decision regarding the LMDs. Thus, as applied to each officials' respective real property interest,
Regulation 18701(b) provides: "[i]n general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable."³

Materiality:

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. As relevant to the facts here,

________________________
² Effects on an official's personal finances are not considered separately from the effect on an official's interest
in real property. (Regulation 18702.5(c).) Thus, we do not consider the personal financial effects rule further.

³ Regulation 18701(b) also provides a non-exhaustive list of six factors that may be considered in the
"reasonably foreseeable" determination.

File No. A-19-020
Page No. 4

Regulation 18702.2⁴ provides that the financial effect of the decisions on the officials' respective real property interest is material if the decision:

• Involves property located 500 feet or less from the property line of the parcel unless there is clear and convincing evidence that the decision will not have any measurable impact on the official's property (Regulation 18702.2(a)(7).)

• Involves property located more than 500 feet but less than 1,000 feet from the property line of the parcel, and the decision would change the parcel's:
(A) Development potential
(B) Income producing potential
(C) Highest and best use
(D) Character by substantially altering traffic levels, intensity of use, parking, view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, or
(E) Market value. (Regulation 18702.2(a)(8).)

• The financial effect of a governmental decision on a parcel of real property in which an official has a financial interest involving property 1,000 feet or more from the property line of the official's property is presumed not to be material. This presumption may be rebutted with clear and convincing evidence the governmental decision would have a substantial effect on the official's property. (Regulation 18702.2(b).)

You indicated that the potential changes to the landscapes near the Councilmembers' property include removal of several trees if additional General Fund resources are not allocated, maintaining the status quo of the trees if sufficient General Fund resources are allocated, or enhancement of the existing landscape with drought tolerant improvements if significant General Fund resources are allocated. None of the options will involve the addition of trees. Furthermore, none of the home owners' special assessments will be impacted by the decision.

Deputy Mayor Dave Grosch.

You have not provided a precise measurement to Deputy Mayor Grosch's property. Assuming the property line of the Deputy Mayor's residence is 1,000 feet or more from the closest potential landscape change, the effect on the Deputy Mayor's real property interest is presumed not to be material. This presumption has not been rebutted with clear and convincing to the contrary. Accordingly, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the City Council's governmental decision will have a material effect on Deputy Mayor Grosch's property. If the property line is less than 1,000 feet, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the removal of dying trees or the addition of drought tolerant enhancements to the nearest impacted landscape would change the Deputy Mayor's parcel's development potential, income producing potential, highest and best use, character, or market value. Accordingly, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material effect on the Deputy Mayor's property.

_______________

⁴ The Commission adopted amendments to Regulation 18702.2 at its January 17, 2019 meeting, and this analysis applies that regulation as amended.

File No. A-19-020
Page No. 5

Councilmember Barry Leonard.

The property line of Councilmember Leonard's residence is within 500 feet from the closest
potential landscape change. As such, the facts presented must clearly and convincingly evidence
that the decision will not have a measurable impact on the Councilmember's real property interest.

You have indicated that the potential landscape changes are almost entirely located on larger
arterial streets, are outside the Councilmembers' actual neighborhoods themselves, and many
citizens of the City benefit from these landscaping improvements when driving down the impacted
arterial streets. The proposed changes are not visible from Councilmember Leonard's residence and
are relatively minimal as the options before the City include (1) maintaining the current level of
funding and stumping trees, (2) providing additional funds solely to maintain existing trees, or (3)
providing additional funds for drought tolerant improvements with no additional trees. Moreover,
based upon the maps provided, Councilmember Leonard's residence is two streets away from the
proposed changes separated by a small cul-de-sac.

Based upon the facts provided, it is unlikely that the minimal proposed changes along large
arterial streets would alter the character or the neighborhood in a manner that affects the value of a
property that is not adjacent to the landscaping change and in a residential subdivision somewhat
separated from the arterial street. Thus, the facts presented evidence it is not reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a measurable impact on Councilmember Leonard's property.

Councilmember John Mullin.

The property line of Councilmember Mullin's residence is located more than 500 feet but
less than 1,000 feet from the closest potential landscape change. For the same reasons as stated for
Deputy Mayor Dave Grosch, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
effect on Councilmember Mullin's property.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Ryan O'Connor
Counsel, Legal Division

RPOC:jgl

Councilmembers' financial interest in their residences.

The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly

governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. As relevant to the facts here, 
2 Effects on an official's personal finances are not considered separately from the effect on an official's interest 
in real property. (Regulation 18702.S(c).) Thus, we do not consider the personal financial effects rule further. 
3 Regulation 1870 I (b) also provides a non-exhaustive list of six factors that may be considered in the

Regulation 18702.24 provides that the financial effect of the decisions on the officials' respective

• 
Involves property located 500 feet or less from the property line of the parcel unless there is

• 
Involves property located more than 500 feet but less than 1,000 feet from the property line 
of the parcel, and the decision would change the parcel's: 
(A) Development potential 
(B) Income producing potential 
(C) Highest and best use 
(D) Character by substantially altering traffic levels, intensity of use, parking, view,

feet, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the removal of dying trees or the addition of draught 
tolerant enhancements to the nearest impacted landscape would change the Deputy Mayor's
